focus: Tag-Question Intonation — Precision Practice for Nuanced Social Meaning
focus_ar: تنغيم الأسئلة الذيلية — تدريب دقيق للمعنى الاجتماعي الدقيق
arabic_challenge: Week 6 introduced the basic rising/falling contrast for tag questions. This week goes deeper into the social nuance: the SAME tag question can express genuine curiosity, gentle confirmation-seeking, sarcasm, or mild challenge, depending entirely on subtle intonation shifts. Blueprint §5.4 explicitly separates 'confirming' (falling) from 'genuine' (rising) tag questions — mastering the finer gradations between them is a mark of advanced fluency.

target_sounds: falling tag: confident confirmation (near-certain of the answer)

isolation.instructions: A falling tag question signals you're almost certain of the answer and are simply inviting agreement — this is the most common tag pattern in casual conversation.
isolation.instructions_ar: السؤال الذيلي المنخفض بيدل إنك متأكد تقريبًا من الإجابة وبس بتطلب موافقة — ده النمط الأكثر شيوعًا في المحادثة العادية.
practice: Nice weather today, isn't it? (falling — obviously true, just chatting)
You're a doctor, aren't you? (falling — fairly confident guess)

sentence_practice: This is a great restaurant, don't you think?
daily_drills.record_this: Nice weather today, isn't it? You're a doctor, aren't you? This is a great restaurant, don't you think?
daily_drills.self_check: Did your pitch fall confidently at the end, matching a near-certain, casual social comment?

isolation.instructions: A rising tag question signals genuine uncertainty — you actually don't know the answer and are truly asking.
isolation.instructions_ar: السؤال الذيلي المرتفع بيدل على شك حقيقي — انت فعلاً ما تعرفش الإجابة وبتسأل بجدية.
practice: You haven't seen my keys, have you? (rising — genuinely hoping/unsure)
This flight isn't delayed, is it? (rising — genuinely worried/unsure)

sentence_practice: You haven't seen my keys, have you?
This flight isn't delayed, is it?
They didn't cancel the meeting, did they?
daily_drills.record_this: You haven't seen my keys, have you? This flight isn't delayed, is it? They didn't cancel the meeting, did they?
daily_drills.self_check: Did your pitch genuinely rise, conveying real uncertainty and concern, not a flat or confident tone?

target_sounds: sarcastic/challenging tag: exaggerated falling with sharp emphasis

isolation.instructions: A sharply falling tag, often with heavy emphasis on the main statement, can signal sarcasm or a mild challenge — this requires careful, context-appropriate use.
isolation.instructions_ar: سؤال ذيلي بانخفاض حاد، غالبًا مع تأكيد قوي على التصريح الأساسي، ممكن يدل على سخرية أو تحدٍ خفيف — ده يحتاج استخدام حذر ومناسب للسياق.
practice: Oh, you're an EXPERT, aren't you? (sharp fall, sarcastic)
You KNEW that already, didn't you? (sharp fall, mild challenge)

pair: you're an EXPERT, aren't you? (sarcastic, sharp fall)
you're an expert, aren't you? (neutral, genuine confirmation)

sentence_practice: Oh, you're an EXPERT, aren't you?
You KNEW that already, didn't you?
That was YOUR idea, wasn't it?
daily_drills.record_this: Oh, you're an EXPERT, aren't you? You KNEW that already, didn't you? That was YOUR idea, wasn't it?
daily_drills.self_check: Caution: this tone can sound rude if misused. Did you clearly distinguish this sharp, sarcastic pattern from the neutral confirming tag in Day 1?

target_sounds: contrasting all 3 patterns side by side

isolation.instructions: Practice the SAME tag question phrase with all 3 different intonations (confident-falling, genuinely-uncertain-rising, sarcastic-sharp-falling) to feel the full range.
isolation.instructions_ar: تدرب على نفس عبارة السؤال الذيلي بالثلاث تنغيمات المختلفة عشان تحس بالمدى الكامل.
practice: You're coming, aren't you? (sarcastic, doubting them)

sentence_practice: You're coming, aren't you? (say this 3 different ways)
This is fine, isn't it? (say this 3 different ways)
daily_drills.record_this: You're coming, aren't you? (confident version) — You're coming, aren't you? (genuinely unsure version) — You're coming, aren't you? (sarcastic version).
daily_drills.self_check: Could you produce all 3 clearly distinct versions of the exact same words, purely through intonation?

target_sounds: tag questions in spontaneous social conversation

isolation.instructions: Practice a spontaneous 30-45 second casual conversation with your buddy (or imagined), naturally using at least 2 different tag question intonations appropriately.
isolation.instructions_ar: تدرب على محادثة عفوية عادية لمدة ٣٠-٤٥ ثانية مع صاحبك (أو متخيل)، باستخدام طبيعي لنمطين مختلفين على الأقل من تنغيم السؤال الذيلي بشكل مناسب.
practice: Have a casual conversation touching on weather, plans, or opinions, using natural tag questions.

pair: natural conversation with appropriate tag intonation

daily_drills.record_this: (Free speech: casual conversation using at least 2 different tag question intonations naturally.)
daily_drills.self_check: Did the tag questions' intonation match your actual intended meaning in each case?

daily_drills.target_sounds: Review: confident-falling, genuine-rising, sarcastic-sharp, side-by-side contrast, spontaneous use
challenge_sentences: Nice weather, isn't it? You haven't seen my umbrella, have you? Oh, you're an EXPERT on weather now, aren't you?
This is a good plan, don't you think? You're not worried, are you? You KNEW this all along, didn't you?
That's a great idea, isn't it? You didn't forget, did you? Oh, you're SO organized, aren't you?
daily_drills.record_this: Nice weather, isn't it? You haven't seen my umbrella, have you? Oh, you're an EXPERT on weather now, aren't you?
daily_drills.self_check: Did each tag question in this passage correctly convey its intended tone — casual, genuine, or sarcastic?

test_yourself.instructions: Record a short natural dialogue (both sides, played by you) using at least 3 tag questions with clearly different, appropriate intonations.
test_yourself.passage: (Create a short imagined dialogue between two friends discussing weekend plans, using at least one confident-falling tag, one genuinely-uncertain-rising tag, and optionally one sarcastic tag if it fits naturally.)

sounds_to_check.confident falling tag: check for a casual, confirming tag
sounds_to_check.genuine rising tag: check for a real question/uncertainty tag
sounds_to_check.sarcastic/challenging tag (optional): check if used, that it's clearly distinct from the others

scoring_guide.90_100: All tag intonation types produced clearly, correctly, and appropriately for their intended social meaning.
scoring_guide.70_89: Most patterns correct; occasional ambiguity between confident and genuine tags.
scoring_guide.50_69: Tags produced with a single, flat intonation regardless of intended meaning.
scoring_guide.below_50: Tag questions avoided or consistently misapplied — go back and repeat Days 1-4.

for_arabic_speakers: This week refines Week 6's basic tag-question introduction into genuinely nuanced, socially-aware usage — this is advanced, native-level precision work.
Use the sarcastic pattern (Day 3) sparingly and only once you're confident about the social context — misjudged sarcasm can create unintended offense.
This is one of the clearest markers of near-native fluency: the ability to convey different social meanings through pitch alone, with identical words.
supplementary_notes.recording_requirement: Record Day 5 and Day 7 — these spontaneous/dialogue recordings are excellent material for peer feedback on social nuance in the Level 2 community channels.
